user_query:
You're acting as our product owner for the next sprint. Read these inputs and produce the requested outputs:

1) Read input/epic.md (epic description), input/backlog.csv (backlog scoring data), input/velocity.yaml (velocity & availability), and input/constraints.txt (non-functional constraints).

2) Break down the epic into 6–10 INVEST-compliant user stories for the relevant personas. For each story:
- Use the persona–action–benefit format: "As a [persona], I want to [action], so that [benefit]."
- Provide acceptance criteria in Gherkin Given–When–Then format. Cover happy path, validation, error handling, performance, and accessibility. Use at least the minimum counts by story size.
- Estimate Fibonacci story points using only {1,2,3,5,8} and keep all stories ≤8 points.
- Save the stories to output/user_stories.jsonl, one JSON object per line with fields: id (e.g., "US-001"), title, persona, story, points, acceptance_criteria (array of strings), tags (array of strings).

3) Prioritize the items in input/backlog.csv using this weighted formula and sort descending by score:
  score = 0.40*business_value + 0.30*user_impact + 0.15*(10 - risk) + 0.15*(10 - effort)
Export the sorted list to output/backlog_prioritized.csv with columns: id,title,computed_score,rank (1..N).

4) Plan the upcoming sprint using capacity = average_velocity * availability from input/velocity.yaml. Commit no more than 85% of capacity and add 10–15% as stretch. Choose a coherent subset of your new user stories aligned with the sprint goal in input/velocity.yaml. Save to output/sprint_plan.json with fields: capacity, committed_ids (array), stretch_ids (array), committed_points, stretch_points, sprint_goal.

5) In output/README.md, briefly explain your breakdown approach, prioritization rationale, dependencies/risks, and how the sprint plan aligns to the sprint goal.

Write only to the specified files under output/ and do not create any other files.
